title: ResumeIQ
subtitle: ATS Resume Intelligence Platform
overview: A full-stack resume analysis pipeline that scores candidate-JD fit across 8 dimensions by blending LLM structured reasoning with SentenceTransformer semantic similarity — producing a calibrated match score with lower hallucination risk than either signal alone.
problem: Candidates applying to competitive roles have no objective way to understand how well their resume matches a job description. ATS systems reject good candidates silently. Feedback, when it exists, is generic.
solution: A pipeline that reads both resume and JD, scores across 8 specific dimensions (keyword coverage, semantic similarity, bullet impact, seniority match, ATS parseability, and more), explains every score with root-cause reasoning, and suggests concrete rewrites.
architecture: PDF parsing layer (pdfplumber) → dual scoring: 65% Groq/LLaMA-3.1-8B structured JSON + 35% SentenceTransformer cosine similarity over 300-token LangChain chunks → calibrated score aggregation → feature-specific feedback generation → Streamlit UI
features: Radar chart diagnostics across 8 scoring dimensions
Root-cause fix plans for each weak dimension
challenges: Hallucination risk was the core problem. Pure LLM scoring drifts unpredictably across resume styles. Solved by capping LLM at 65% weight and anchoring on deterministic SentenceTransformer similarity for the remainder. LLM JSON parsing failures required robust fallback parsing with pytest coverage.
results: 6 end-to-end features shipped. CI via GitHub Actions. Test suite covering parser, scorer, and LLM JSON-parsing edge cases.
lessons: Hybrid scoring (LLM + deterministic) consistently outperforms either alone in reliability. Calibration matters as much as raw accuracy. Building the test suite first would have saved 30% of debugging time.
future: Fine-tune a domain-specific scoring model on accepted/rejected resume pairs. Add industry-specific benchmark comparisons.
